key_insight: The paper argues that AI systems can develop native, self-consistent meta-architecture by converting application-layer cognitive control into three mechanisms: structural tension, offline recurrent processing, and inference-time plasticity.
engineering_relevance: For computational science and AI systems design, the framework suggests a governance-friendly way to manage memory, adaptation, and architectural change without retraining model weights.
method: Define structural tension as an endogenous loss from conflict between new inputs and existing manifold topology.
Use an offline recurrent loop to let the model self-process conflicts in a sandboxed resting state.
Apply inference-time plasticity operators to reconfigure context topology while preserving auditability, reversibility, and continuity.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a theoretical framework with operational definitions, reconfiguration operators, falsification criteria, and a worked example rather than empirical benchmark results.
watch_item: The approach remains conceptual and its practicality depends on whether inference-time topology changes can be made stable, auditable, and robust across real deployments.
paper_title: From Application-Layer Simulation to Native Meta-Architecture: Structural Tension as an Endogenous Driver for Heterogeneous AI Evolution
